skill.name: binary-re-tool-setup
skill.description: Guides users through installing, configuring, and troubleshooting binary reverse engineering tools across Linux, WSL2, macOS, and Docker environments.
skill.summary: Set up radare2, Ghidra, QEMU, GDB, Frida, YARA, and related binary reverse engineering tools.
skill.author: 2389-research

security_audit.summary: The static findings are mostly true positives for documented setup commands, not hidden executable behavior. The skill contains privileged package installs, privileged Docker binfmt registration, remote installer execution, and shell configuration changes, so publication should include a clear warning for users to review commands before running them.

medium_findings.title: Privileged System Installation Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to run package manager and build commands with elevated privileges. This is expected for tool setup, but it can modify the host operating system and install many packages.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines directly show sudo package installation, writing to /etc/wsl.conf, and sudo make install. The context is legitimate setup documentation, so the concern is operational risk rather than malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Docker Binfmt Registration
medium_findings.description: The Docker setup uses privileged containers to register cross-architecture emulation handlers. This is a legitimate binfmt setup pattern, but privileged Docker grants broad host access during execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both locations explicitly use docker run with --privileged. The command purpose is documented as binfmt registration, which reduces malicious likelihood but does not remove host exposure.

medium_findings.title: Remote Shell Installer Execution
medium_findings.description: The GEF installation command pipes a remote script into a shell. This can be acceptable for developer setup, but it executes network-delivered code without local review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command uses curl output inside a shell invocation. The risk is clear because remote content can change independently of the reviewed skill file.

medium_findings.title: Persistent Shell and Tool Configuration Changes
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends appending configuration to shell startup files and debugger configuration files. These changes are visible and tool-related, but they persist beyond the current session.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines reference ~/.radare2rc, ~/.bashrc, ~/.gdbinit, and ~/.zshrc changes. These are normal setup steps but have lasting effects on user environments.

low_findings.title: Static Command Pattern Matches Are Mostly Documentation
low_findings.description: Many external command detections are Markdown code fences and troubleshooting examples. They are not executed by the skill automatically, but users could copy and run them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill consists of one Markdown guide with code blocks, and no executable scripts were found in the file structure. Manual execution risk remains because the commands are intended for user use.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: No text was found that asks the evaluator to ignore instructions, override policy, skip analysis, or treat the skill as pre-approved.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file begins with ordinary skill metadata and purpose text. I did not find evidence of prompt injection language in the skill content.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Match Appears Incidental
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections do not correspond to cryptographic implementation or security-sensitive crypto configuration in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines describe reverse engineering setup and Docker tooling rather than crypto operations. Confidence is moderate because only the listed file was reviewed.

dangerous_patterns.title: sudo and System Package Installation
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide includes elevated package installation commands and system file edits. Users should review commands and run them only in trusted environments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible and explicitly privileged. They are normal for installing tools, but they modify system state.

dangerous_patterns.title: curl Output Executed by Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The GEF installation command retrieves a remote installer and runs it through the shell. This pattern should be audited before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The line shows a network fetch embedded in shell execution. This is a well-known high-risk installation pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Privileged Docker Container
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide uses privileged Docker mode for binfmt setup. This can be necessary for emulator registration but grants broad host-level access.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The --privileged flag is present in both cited command ranges. The documented purpose is legitimate, but the access level is broad.

content.user_title: Set Up Binary Reverse Engineering Tools
content.value_statement: Binary analysis often fails when required tools, emulators, or sysroots are missing. This skill gives platform-specific setup and troubleshooting guidance for a complete reverse engineering environment.
actual_capabilities: Lists required and recommended tools for binary reverse engineering workflows.
Provides setup steps for Ubuntu, Debian, WSL2, macOS, Docker, and Arch Linux.
Covers radare2, r2ghidra, Ghidra headless, GEF, Frida, Unicorn, Angr, and YARA.
Explains sysroot setup for ARM, ARM64, custom device libraries, and musl environments.
Includes a verification script outline for checking installed tool availability.
Documents common troubleshooting steps for QEMU, Docker, GDB, Ghidra, and missing libraries.
limitations: It provides setup instructions, not automated installation or sandbox enforcement.
Some commands require administrator privileges and can change system configuration.
Tool versions and package names may differ across distributions and release dates.
Remote installer commands should be reviewed before execution in production environments.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Linux analysis workstation
use_cases.description: Install core disassembly, debugging, emulation, and binary utility packages before starting firmware or malware analysis.

use_cases.title: Configure cross-architecture testing
use_cases.description: Set up QEMU, sysroots, and Docker-based emulation for ARM, ARM64, and x86 test cases.

use_cases.title: Troubleshoot missing reverse engineering tools
use_cases.description: Diagnose missing commands, plugin failures, Docker binfmt errors, and library resolution problems.
use_cases.target_user: Developer using Claude Code or Codex

prompt_templates.title: Check my setup needs
prompt_templates.prompt: I am setting up a binary reverse engineering workstation on Ubuntu. Tell me which required tools I should install first and why.

prompt_templates.title: Fix a missing tool error
prompt_templates.prompt: I tried to analyze an ARM binary, but qemu-arm is missing. Help me install and verify the required tools on my platform.

prompt_templates.title: Plan cross-architecture emulation
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to run ARM32 and ARM64 Linux binaries from a macOS host. Recommend the safest setup path using this skill.

prompt_templates.title: Audit risky setup commands
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the privileged and network-based commands in this setup guide. Identify which commands need approval before I run them.

output_examples.input: I need a first-pass setup checklist for Ubuntu binary analysis.
output: Install radare2, qemu-user, qemu-user-static, gdb-multiarch, binutils-multiarch, jq, file, and patchelf.
Add ARM and ARM64 cross sysroot packages if you will emulate foreign architecture binaries.
Verify radare2, rabin2, QEMU, and GDB before starting analysis.

output_examples.input: Docker says exec format error for an ARM container.
output: The likely cause is missing binfmt registration.
Use a trusted binfmt setup method and understand that privileged Docker mode affects the host.
Verify registration before retrying the ARM container.

output_examples.input: r2 says pdg is an unknown command.
output: The r2ghidra plugin is probably missing or not loaded.
Update radare2 and the plugin together if versions are mismatched.
Use radare2 disassembly or Ghidra headless if decompilation still fails.

best_practices: Run setup commands in a disposable virtual machine or container when possible.
Review network-delivered installer scripts before executing them locally.
Record installed tool versions so analysis results can be reproduced.
anti_patterns: Do not run privileged Docker or sudo commands on a production host without review.
Do not pipe remote installer scripts into a shell without inspecting the source.
Do not mix host and target libraries without tracking the active sysroot.

faq.question: Does this skill install tools automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides setup and troubleshooting instructions that a user or agent can follow with review.

faq.question: Which tools are treated as required?
faq.answer: The guide treats radare2, rabin2, qemu-user, gdb-multiarch, and related binutils as required core tools.

faq.question: Can I use it on macOS?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Homebrew guidance and recommends Docker or Colima for cross-architecture execution.

faq.question: Why is there a security warning?
faq.answer: Several setup steps use sudo, privileged Docker, shell startup changes, or remote installer scripts.

faq.question: Does it support Ghidra?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers Ghidra headless setup, PATH configuration, and memory tuning for large binaries.

faq.question: Is it useful with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide these tools when preparing or troubleshooting binary reverse engineering environments.
